content.problemStatement:
Anakin's spacecraft is being attacked by Buzz droids. Again. Buzz droids intend to disable rather than destroy their targets. They achieve this by introducing bugs in the program of the underlying software.



One important module of every navigation controller is the Floyd-Warshall algorithm. Here is how the code for Floyd-Warshall looks like:


`
n <- number of vertices in the graph

adj[n][n] <- adjacency matrix(0/1) of a simple undirected graph

connected[n][n] <- 2D matrix of size n * n

for (i=1; i<=n; i++)
  for (j=1; j<=n; j++)
    connected[i][j] = adj[i][j]

for (p=1; p<=n; p++)    // runs only up to n-k after reprogramming
  for (i=1; i<=n; i++)
    for (j=1; j<=n; j++)
      connected[i][j] = (connected[i][j]) 
			|| (i!=j && connected[i][p] && connected[p][j])

return connected
`

Due to reprogramming by Buzz droids, now the outer loop on **p** runs only from **1** to **n-k** (both inclusive).


Your task is to find the number of simple undirected unweighted graphs for which the reprogrammed code still returns the correct answer. Two graphs are considered different if their adjacency matrices are not identical.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of a line with two integers n and k separated by a space.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of simple undirected graphs for which the buggy Floyd‑Warshall code returns the correct transitive closure, modulo 10^9+7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For (1,1) there is only one graph. For (2,2) both possible graphs (edge present or not) are valid. For (3,1) six out of eight possible graphs satisfy the condition.
When k >= n, the outer loop does nothing, so only the graph with no edges yields the correct closure for n=1. For n=3,k=3 the answer is 8.
With k = n, no intermediate vertices are considered; the answer equals 2^{n-1} * 2^{n-2} * ... * 2^{0} = 64.

solution.approach:
The original Floyd‑Warshall computes the transitive closure by considering every vertex as an intermediate node. If the outer loop runs only up to n‑k, vertices with index > n‑k are never used as intermediates. For the algorithm to still be correct, any pair of vertices that need a path through a missing vertex must already be directly connected. This forces a specific structure: for each vertex i <= n‑k, its adjacency to the earlier vertices can be arbitrary (2^{i-1} possibilities) and it may also be isolated, giving (1 + 2^{i-1}) choices. For vertices i > n‑k, they cannot be used as intermediates, so they must have no edges to vertices with larger index; only edges to earlier vertices are allowed, giving 2^{n-i} possibilities. Multiplying these independent choices for all i yields the answer. The formula is:
    ans = Π_{i=1}^{n-k} (1 + 2^{i-1}) * Π_{i=n-k+1}^{n} 2^{n-i}  (mod 1e9+7).
We compute powers of two with fast modular exponentiation to avoid overflow.
solution.timeComplexity: O(n log MOD) per test case (log MOD for each modular exponentiation). Since n ≤ 180, this easily fits the limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides input variables.
